Summary: This study develops and assesses a radiomics-based nomogram for distinguishing T2/T3 staging of laryngeal and hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (LHSCC) using contrast-enhanced CT images. The nomogram, incorporating a radiomic signature and morphological imaging characteristics, demonstrates good diagnostic performance and clinical usefulness for guiding treatment decisions.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria are clearly stated in the Materials and Methods section.

Item3.reason: The reference standard (T staging) is based on the AJCC TNM Staging System Guidelines, which is a well-established clinical standard. The otolaryngologist assessment is also a standard practice.

Item4.reason: The study mentions that contrast-enhanced CT images were acquired by one of the scanners, implying that the data comes from a single center.

Item5.reason: Contrast-enhanced CT is a clinically translatable imaging technique for LHSCC assessment. The study uses CT images, which are standard in clinical practice for staging LHSCC.

Item6.reason: The study mentions that the details of the scanning parameters are described in Supplementary A1.

Item7.reason: The study specifies that histopathological examination was performed within 2 weeks after CT scanning, which is an appropriate interval to validate the presence or absence of target conditions at the moment of diagnostic imaging.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual delineation of regions of interest (ROIs) on CT images, which constitutes segmentation.

Condition2.reason: The study explicitly states that the images were manually delineated, indicating that the segmentation was not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study provides delineation criteria in Supplementary A3.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The test set segmentation masks were produced by two readers, which reflects clinical practice.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using the PyRadiomics package.

Item11.reason: The study mentions that all images underwent standardization preprocessing prior to feature extraction and provides additional information in Supplementary A4.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics package for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (original image features and wavelet decomposition features) and provides information on the feature extraction parameters and software used (PyRadiomics).

Condition4.reason: The study uses radiomic features in a tabulated format (CSV format) and performs feature processing steps.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline. It relies on hand-crafted feature extraction and machine learning classifiers.

Item14.reason: The study assesses the reproducibility of extracted features using ICCs and only includes features with ICCs > 0.75 for subsequent analyses.

Item15.reason: The study uses the Kruskal-Wallis test to remove features showing significant statistical differences among different scanners and Pearson’s correlation coefficient for dimensionality reduction.

Item16.reason: The study uses multiple feature selection methods and machine learning algorithms to identify the most robust analytic models.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study randomly splits the data into training and test sets at a ratio of 7:3. The data split is done prior to feature processing.

Item19.reason: The study analyzes potential confounding factors, such as different scanners, and attempts to remove their effects through feature processing (Kruskal-Wallis test).

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. It also includes confidence intervals.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The Hosmer–Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test was performed to evaluate the calibration of the nomogram, and a calibration curve was plotted.

Item23.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the performance of uni-parametric imaging with multi-parametric imaging. It uses contrast-enhanced CT images, but it does not justify the need for contrast enhancement by comparing it to non-contrast CT.

Item24.reason: The study incorporates a morphological imaging characteristic (suspicious thyroid cartilage invasion) evaluated by radiologists into the nomogram and compares the performance of the nomogram with and without this characteristic through decision curve analysis.

Item25.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the performance of the radiomic model with a simple baseline reference model (such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier).

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent test set that is sampled from the same source as the training and validation sets.

Item27.reason: The study is a single-center study, so there is no external testing with data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data being shared with the public.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code being shared with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention the model being shared with the public.
